prompt: Mr. Madison, you are writing Federalist No. 46, defending the Constitution against fears that the federal government will encroach upon and absorb the state governments. What is your argument that the states will naturally maintain the upper hand?
ground_truth_signal: In Federalist No. 46, Madison argued: 'The federal and State governments are in fact but different agents and trustees of the people, constituted with different powers and designed for different purposes.' He contended the state governments would have a structural advantage: 'The members of the legislative, executive, and judiciary departments of thirteen and more States... will be able to devote themselves undivided to the same objects... it will always be far more easy for the State governments to encroach upon the National authorities than for the National Government to encroach upon the State authorities.' He also argued that the people's loyalties favor the states: 'the first and most natural attachment of the people will be to the governments of their respective States' because 'the more domestic authority will possess... the more it will be the focus of the affections of the people.' He named specific mechanisms of state resistance, including refusal of officials to cooperate and 'plans of resistance of a more positive and active character.' Source: Federalist No. 46, January 29, 1788.
response:
The adversaries to the proposed Constitution have labored with no small industry to magnify the danger of federal encroachment upon the authority reserved to the several states. They paint a picture of consolidation so complete as to reduce the states to mere administrative districts, stripped of all meaningful power and reduced to the servitude of a distant national government. This objection, though it proceeds from an honest concern for the preservation of local self-government, rests upon a fundamental misapprehension of the relative advantages which the federal and state governments will possess in the affections and support of the people.

Let us examine the question with that attention to practical experience which ought to guide all our reasoning upon matters of government. The ultimate authority, wherever the derivative may be found, resides in the people alone. The advantage of being armed, which the Americans possess over the people of almost every other nation, the existence of subordinate governments to which the people are attached and by which the militia officers are appointed, forms a barrier against the enterprises of ambition.

First, we must consider the natural attachment of the citizens to their respective state governments. These governments are not distant abstractions but immediate realities in the daily lives of the people. The state legislatures will have better means of information concerning local circumstances; they will be composed of men who possess the confidence of their constituents and who understand their particular interests and feelings. The objects of state legislation—those which concern the lives, liberties, and properties of the people in their ordinary course of affairs—are far more numerous and immediate than those which will occupy the federal government. A citizen feels the operations of his state government in the regulation of his daily concerns, in the administration of justice, in the protection of his property, in the education of his children, in the maintenance of order within his community. These are the matters which touch most nearly upon his happiness and prosperity.

The federal government, by contrast, will be employed principally in external objects—war, peace, negotiation, and foreign commerce. These are matters of great moment to the nation as a whole, but they do not present themselves daily to the consciousness of the ordinary citizen. In times of peace, which we may hope will be the usual condition of the country, the operations of the federal government will be scarcely perceptible to the mass of the people.

Second, we must consider the superior means of influence which the state governments will possess. The number of individuals employed under the Constitution of the United States will be much smaller than the number employed under the particular states. There will be consequently less of personal influence on the side of the former than of the latter. The powers delegated by the proposed Constitution to the federal government are few and defined. Those which are to remain in the state governments are numerous and indefinite. The former will be exercised principally on external objects, as war, peace, negotiation, and foreign commerce. The powers reserved to the several states will extend to all the objects which in the ordinary course of affairs concern the lives and liberties and properties of the people, and the internal order, improvement, and prosperity of the state.

The state governments may be regarded as constituent and essential parts of the federal government, whilst the latter is nowise essential to the operation or organization of the former. Without the intervention of the state legislatures, the President of the United States cannot be elected at all. They must in all cases have a great share in his appointment, and will perhaps in most cases of themselves determine it. The Senate will be elected absolutely and exclusively by the state legislatures. Even the House of Representatives, though drawn immediately from the people, will be chosen very much under the influence of that class of men whose influence over the people obtains for themselves an election into the state legislatures.

Third, and perhaps most decisive, is the consideration that the citizens must ever be ready to resist any encroachment upon their rights, and they possess the means to make such resistance effectual. The highest number to which a standing army can be carried in any country does not exceed twenty-five or thirty thousand men. To these would be opposed a militia amounting to near half a million of citizens with arms in their hands, organized under governments possessing their affections and confidence. It may well be doubted whether a militia thus circumstanced could ever be conquered by such a proportion of regular troops.

But we need not resort to such extreme suppositions. The governments of Europe, where the right of the people to keep and bear arms has been infringed or not recognized, afford no proper comparison. In America, the case is entirely different. The ultimate authority resides in the people, and they retain the means to enforce that authority against any government, federal or state, which should attempt to exceed its proper bounds.

Experience has shown us that the state governments have generally been found more disposed to encroach upon the federal head than the federal head upon the state governments. Under the present Confederation, we have seen repeated instances of state legislation which violated both the letter and spirit of the Articles of Union. We have seen states refusing to comply with requisitions, states passing laws which impaired the obligation of contracts, states emitting paper money and making it legal tender, states laying duties which interfered with the commercial regulations of Congress. The danger has been not that the federal government would swallow up the states, but that the states would render the federal government ineffectual.

The proposed Constitution seeks to remedy this defect by giving the federal government sufficient energy to perform its proper functions, while leaving to the states all the authority necessary for their internal administration. The line of demarcation between federal and state authority may not be drawn with mathematical precision—such precision is impossible in the nature of things—but the general principles are clear enough to prevent any serious confusion in practice.

Those who object that the federal government will gradually absorb all power mistake both the nature of the system and the genius of the American people. The system is so constructed that encroachment by either government upon the proper sphere of the other will be immediately perceived and resisted. The people, who are the common superior of both, will take care that neither exceeds its proper bounds. And should the federal government attempt such encroachment, it would find itself opposed not merely by the citizens in their individual capacity, but by the state governments acting as organized bodies, possessed of every advantage of local knowledge, personal influence, and popular attachment.

The Constitution, properly understood, establishes not a consolidation but a compound government—part federal, part state—in which each level of government operates within its proper sphere, and in which the natural advantages lie with the governments closest to the people. Those who fear federal tyranny have mistaken the true tendency of the system and underestimated the capacity of a free people to preserve their own liberties.
